turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in No. 90-5551, Edward Harold Schad, Jr. v. Arizona. Ms. Young.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Edward Schad was convicted of first degree murder and sentenced to death after a trial at which the evidence presented was that the victim was strangled, and that sometime later Mr. Schad had possession of some of the victim's property. Two rules Arizona mechanically applied in Mr. Schad's case allowed the prosecution to obtain this verdict based on this then evidence. The question -- both issues presented in this case presents is whether that verdict obtained by the rigid application of these two rules is constitutionally reliable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This was a verdict in the guilt phase, Ms. Young?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Chief Justice. Although I'm going to discuss each rule separately, that is not how they worked at Mr. Schad's capital trial. These two rules, Arizona's one form of verdict rule and Arizona's no lesser included offenses for felony murder rule worked together, mutually reinforcing one another and increasing the risk that the verdict returned against Mr. Schad was erroneous. I will first address the second issue we present in our brief, Arizona's rule that there are no lesser included offenses of felony murder, because it presents a more narrow constitutional issue and it is controlled by Beck v. Alabama. The prosecutor's case for felony murder was built circumstantially. No direct evidence tied Mr. Schad to the homicide of the victim. The only evidence connecting him to that crime was his possession of the victim's property. The prosecutor argued that because he had possession of the property, the jury could find that he robbed the victim and that he killed the victim during the course of that robbery, and was therefore guilty of felony murder. The defense used that same evidence to argue their defense that, because he had possession of the property but because there was no evidence directly tieing him to the homicide, that perhaps the jury could find him a thief, perhaps a robber, but not a murderer. Because of Arizona's mandatory rule precluding lesser included offenses for felony murder, the judge refused to instruct the jury on the offense of robbery or on the offense of theft, which under Arizona law is a lesser included offense of robbery.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Young, did the judge give any lesser included offense instruction at all in this case?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: The judge did give an instruction for second degree murder which was a lesser included offense instruction for premeditated murder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And do you think that requires, as a matter of Federal constitutional law, any number of lesser included offense instructions if — if the evidence makes them out.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes. Beck requires that the jury be given all rational lesser included offenses where supported by the evidence. Now that is not as broad as it may seem. Beck of course is limited to capital cases. And a state can define its offenses in such a manner that they are not a lesser included offense of the greater offense. But that's not what Arizona did here. Mr. Schad was tried for felony murder based a killing which occurred during the course of the — of a robbery. The jury was instructed that it could find Mr.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wasn't that — wasn't the — Beck was confined to capital cases, wasn't it?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's exactly right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so it was to give the jury an option to imposing the death penalty.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, but also giving them an option to return a rational verdict. Beck is based on rationalizing the fact-finding process, not derationalizing the fact-finding process.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you're saying if the jury in the jury room agrees at the outset that first degree premeditated murder has not been proven, at that point, if they — they cannot consistently go to second degree murder?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. Felony murder as defined by Arizona law has two elements, a killing occurring during the course of some statutorily enumerated felony — here, robbery. Those jurors here who had a doubt as to whether Mr. Schad had anything to do with the killing -- and that was the very factual issue in dispute here, and there was considerable doubt here whether he did -- were unable to return a verdict consistent with that doubt, which is exactly what this Court was concerned about in Beck, concerned about letting the jury have a rational option if they are not convinced degree murder that's —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that's the offense.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's true, Your Honor, which — which brings us to our second issue, of course.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As I said.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: But -- but this jury was not just — they weren't given an instruction of this is just first degree murder. They were given instruction of first degree murder is either premeditated murder or felony murder and let me tell you what murder is. They were then instructed on that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But — but in felony murder, the defendant must cause the death in Arizona?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: No, Your Honor, felony murder is basically a strict liability offense in Arizona. The defendant does not have to play any part in the killing whatsoever. It's just a killing occurs during the course of a felony.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And there was no instruction to that effect in this case?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: The —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In this case, there's no instruction that the defendant must have caused the death for there to be a capital conviction?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: No, Your Honor. Now, there was a instruction for premeditated murder that he must have caused it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that for premeditated —

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Not for felony murder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's not -- that's not required in a capital case?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Not in Arizona.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what about the penalty phase?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: At the penalty phase it is performed by a judge. We have judge sentencing only.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know. But what's -- what must he find? That the defendant killed or intended to have a killing?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, he must, in order to be eligible for the death sentence, make an Inman- Tison finding. That's exactly right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that's after sentencing phase.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's at the sentencing phase.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that's just -- the sentencing phase.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But just to be clear about it, there is no Arizona crime of felony murder?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes, there is, Your Honor. There is an Arizona crime of felony murder, and Arizona tries defendants often on — just based on felony murder — just as Arizona defines —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought the defined crime was first degree murder, and there are various ways of proving first degree murder. But there's no separate penalty denominated for felony murder. It's just one way of showing first degree.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: It's — it's under the first degree murder degree statute. That is right, Your Honor. But —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So I think your answer should have been yes.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: A defendant can be tried in Arizona only for felony murder, though. A defendant can be tried in Arizona only for premeditated murder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He can be tried for first degree murder with the State arguing only one theory of first degree murder — to whit, the felony theory — if the State wishes to proceed that way.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. That's right. That guestion of course brings us to the second issue -- our first issue actually in our brief. The —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I'll ask it later I guess, whether you must win on this in order to win on the first.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I do not believe we must win on this issue in order to win on the first at all. The second rule that Arizona applied in Mr. Schad's case is the one form of verdict rule that we have been discussing. That rule applies in a capital case whenever a prosecutor seeks a first degree murder instruction based on either premeditated murder or felony murder. When a prosecutor invokes that rule, he eliminates the defendant's right under the constitution in Arizona to a unanimous jury verdict. This is how that rule worked in Mr. Schad's capital trial. The prosecutor had only two facts on which to obtain a conviction here. The victim was strangled, and Mr. Schad later had possession of some of the victim's property. The prosecutor told the jury that it could find Mr. Schad guilty of first degree murder if it found either premeditated murder or felony murder and that there were two different, factual routes that the jury could take in order to reach a conviction on each of those crimes. The prosecutor first argued the premeditation route. He told the jury that he could find -- that they could find the defendant guilty of premeditated murder if it found one fact — excuse me -- based on one fact, that the victim was strangled. The prosecutor argued that that fact alone showed he premeditated the crime. The prosecutor then argued an entirely different it has with the essential elements of premeditation on this side -- and the prosecutor argued the case this way to the jury. He outlined the elements on the blackboard to the jury. Here they are, three elements of premeditated murder. Here's how you find it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I assume —

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: He strangled the victim.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I assume you would say the same thing if we granted your — your claim in this case. The next step would be, what if the jury disagrees as to what the felony was in felony murder? Six jurors think that the felony was attempted rape. Six jurors think that it was robbery. The individual can't be convicted. Is that right? I mean —

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, where — where do you stop. I mean the next level down, there are various ways of -- of robbery, as Justice Kennedy said. And where does it all end?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: It can end with the capital case. It can end at the first degree murder statute. It does not need to go any further than that. But the way --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I don't understand that. If your theory is right. You — I understood your theory to be it ends with different elements of the offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: With the -- with Justice Kennedy's example, it's the same element, namely entry, that you don't care how he got in if they all thought he broke in. But here you've got two different elements that are mutually exclusive, really. Not necessarily mutually exclusive, but he either robbed or he intended to kill. Those are different elements. I thought that was your theory —

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That is —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- not just capital cases.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, unless -- unless it was defined as an — just an element is a felony. You might semantically say that in Justice Scalia's example that the elements are the same whether it's robbery or rape.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: You might, but that's -- I think a unanimous jury verdict would have to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There are not many indictments —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you don't need — in other words, you can say that and still prevail in your case, because you can say the elements of premeditated murder and felony murder are different.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's right. That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But doesn't -- doesn't that then just raise the further question, why — why isn't it appropriate to analyze the Arizona statute as simply requiring a guilty mental state which may be proven either as premeditation or may be proven as a element in the intent to commit robbery in the course of which there is a killing? Your argument depends on the assumption that there are two different mental states involved, and hence two different elements. If we don't accept that assumption, then your argument falls.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: But — but there are, Your Honor. Under -- as Arizona has defined felony murder, there certainly are two different mental states. The mental state for felony murder has nothing to do -- no connection whatsoever to the homicide. It is a mental state for the underlying felony that cannot be substituted over here for the premeditation when the jury may have found that Mr. Schad had no involvement in the killing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there are many more than two mental states required. I mean there are as many mental states required as there are all the felonies in Arizona plus one more for — for the intentional killing, right?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: For the premeditated murder —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean every felony if it's -- if it requires mens rea, I assume, has a different intent, right? So there are as many intents required under this statute as there are felonies in Arizona you say?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Intents intents that the statute has required for that particular felony, that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, and for -- and for this first degree murder.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's right. We know what the -- we know what the mental state is for that, of course -- the intent and the premeditation for the -- the premeditated murder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's — that's one intent.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then there's not just a second, which is an intent to commit a felony. You — there's a different intent for each one of the felonies, right? So there are 30 different intents here. Who knows?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: There could be. But — but this is not a great burden we're asking the State to take on. All we're asking is that they give the jury separate forms of verdict.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I think it is unless you want to have some special rules for capital cases. And I certainly don't think your logic takes you there. What about that Federal statute that punishes as an accomplice anybody who aids, counsels, procures, abets, commands? Does the jury have to be instructed — do they have to be — it was either commanding or counseling or procuring? They have to all agree on which of the three?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: They — they --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because those are really a different act.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: If in fact the evidence is such that it is, they do have to unanimously agree on the facts essential to that crime. And if those facts are so disparate as they are here and can establish such two entirely different crimes as they do here —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say that's — that's the present state of Federal law? Is that what you're saying? Is -- are you saying that the answer to Justice — that the present state of Federal law is that if a particular statute says aids, abets, counsels, et cetera, that a jury must have a separate form of verdict and unanimously find either one or the other of those?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: No, I —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: I do not know that to be so, Your Honor. I know that there's been at least no decision out of this Court along those lines. The circuit courts have come out various ways when in fact they were faced with the statute that the jury could have understood required — the — the — them to go off on -- the jury to go off on two separate factual routes as it did here. issue for a while — those six people may not have had — believed he had anything to do with the killing but simply was present during a robbery. That is far different from the other six jurors who believed there was premeditation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you — do you say that it must be clear that the jury was unanimous on each -- on whatever theory that -- on one theory or the other?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: On either premeditated murder or felony murder? Yes, I do, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is — what is your case that says that, in a State case, there must unanimous verdicts?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Under Arizona law, the constitution requires that their verdict be unanimous.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but do you have any case here that says it must be unanimous?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: A United States Supreme Court case? No, no, there's no case by this Court that it must be. There are certainly circuit court cases -- the Gipson case

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what makes it -- let's assume that there's — that there's a clear rule here that -- that -- that there may not be unanimous verdicts in the state criminal cases, including capital cases. Let's just assume that.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But Arizona requires it. On what ground would you then say we should reverse this case? It's just because Arizona has this rule, but is it unconstitutional to have a — in a — for Arizona to do it this way?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Well, the possibility that the jury here split evenly --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes .

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: — even violates this Court's minimum requirements.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that's -- you really get down to 6 to 6 thing then?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, or —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would have to be a majority.

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: At least a majority. Here we have them splitting evenly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say you have them. That's your hypothesis, isn't it?

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: That's — yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We don't know how the jury --

turns.name: DENISE I. YOUNG
turns.text: We have no idea here what the jury did. Thank you. I'm going to reserve the rest of my time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Very well, Ms. Young. Mr. Ford, we'll hear now from you.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: What this case presents is the classical death by strangulation/drowning situation that you often see raised in the law schools by the law professors where a victim is found at the bottom of the swimming pool. And when he's pulled out and goes to the morgue and the medical examiner examines him, finds also that the death could have been -- there's conclusive evidence that could have been also strangled as well as drowned. And the question that this case presents is whether the jury is constitutionally required which of the two had happened. In this case, of course, there's not a drowning. My position in this situation is that it is a question of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask you right there? Supposing Arizona had — in your very hypothetical — one statute prohibiting death by — killing by drowning which had a — which was not a capital case, where as death by strangulation was a capital case. Would they then be required to find out which way it was done?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: I -- my assumption was that the death by drowning by a forcible -- it was a killing -- and that the jury ultimately decides that way. If it was an accidental drowning --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no, no. I just said that your two examples as I understood you were death by strangulation and death by drowning, wasn't it?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: My — my presumption should have been that it was death by drowning -- by forcible drowning. And the jury unanimously decided there was a killing. Must they then — are they then constitutionally required to decide whether the killing was by forcible drowning or whether --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And my question to you is — I think to put your question, you have to assume the statute only applied — there's a special crime of killing by drowning. And then would they not have to find that there was killing by drowning?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Under the -- if the statute said killing by drowning?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Then the jury would have to be unanimous in making that finding. However, under the Arizona statute, it does not specify killing by drowning. The Arizona legislature, in its prerogative of defining a tax cheat -- whatever. If I -- you know, if I can pick up nine votes for one or the other, I have a conviction. Can you do that?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: It would depend on how the statute would be written. I was --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I told you how it's written.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a felony.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: If — if you mean that there a robbery independent and we're going to punish him for a -- a homicide, I don't believe that they could do that. If you're saying that he commit a homicide by robbery, then I think the legislature —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, take homicide -- I think you're getting confused by homicide. Take homicide out of it.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a -- it's a new crime called a nonhomicide felony, and it includes every felony in the world except homicide.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right? But they are not elements of it. They are just like the two elements of intent in this statute. Could Arizona do that?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: I believe that Arizona — that the legislature would not be allowed to do that. I believe you'd be running into a due process, a reasonable doubt standard, which —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Uh-huh. Now, why is this different? How do you -- how do you draw the line between that statute and the one you're defending here?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Because you're talking about a traditional category of crime here. You're talking about the legislature has decided to define the offense of homicide or murder in this specific instance. That is a category I believe that the legislature should be allowed to designate as an offense -- call it homicide or call it murder which ever you call it -- and should be allowed to say if you commit a homicide by any of these means, then you have committed the crime of homicide.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what's -- what's the standard that you using in order to answer Justice Scalia's question in which you said that the hypothetical statute he put of a generic felony statute has a due process problem? What is this standard you have to meet in order to be consistent with due process?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: You would have to look at the way this statute is written and determine whether or not the jury is making the determination of the crime that is charged in that case, as opposed to —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but the crime is the one Justice Scalia put. It's -- it is the crime of being a dangerous person. And you can do it by robbing a bank, committing arson, or reckless driving.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: If you define it that way, then you're beyond — I foresee the problem there as — what you're doing is you're giving up the jury's -- the defendant's right to a jury trial and giving it over to the judge merely because the jury is deciding, okay, he did something wrong. Three can find it this way, and six can find it that way, and three the other way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's -- that's the argument in this case.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: I disagree, because in this case --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the difference in the two cases, and what's the standard you use to determine that -- that one is valid and the other is invalid?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: The line that I would draw would be if it falls within one of the traditional type of crimes, then it would be all right for the legislature to say, you have committed a homicide. But they can't throw in and say that you will be guilty of — we will find you under the law guilty of a homicide if you commit speeding in an automobile. That's without --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, would you say there has to be a close conceptual mens rea between the acts that are

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: If — if they had — if they came back with separate verdicts itself, yes, under the Arizona statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Six and six?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: We're talking about the way -- the manner in which the murder was committed as opposed to whether a murder was committed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, so -- so you're -- you're quite content with the assumption that six on one and six on the other would still produce a guilty verdict constitutionally?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: If six jurors found, yes, that there was -- that he killed by premeditation and six found that he commit -- that he killed during the commission of a felony, there's no problem under that. There's no constitutional bar to the legislature defining such a crime. Those are merely different ways.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because -- and you would say the State law rule of unanimity is satisfied, because there's 12 people who voted for first degree murder?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Because 12 people voted for the offense. The focus on a murder crime is on the killing and not on how it was committed. As —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In effect, you're — you're saying then that if there's a 6-6 split on how it's committed, we don't have a due process problem keyed to reasonable doubt? We do not have to say, as a matter of law, you can't have reasonable — you can't have a finding beyond a reasonable doubt even though there's a — there's a split 6 to 6. But if we got a greater number of fractioned possibilities -- if we maybe got three-three-three, it would be an implication for reasonable doubt. Is that what you're saying?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: No, no. If I said that or implied that, I misspoke.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I guess then — let me if I may just ask you one other question, and that is when — when you were — when were answering Justice Kennedy's question, he said, well, what is the due process implication that bothers you in Justice Scalia's hypothesis in which there might be, you know, 30 ways to commit the generic felony. And I thought you said that the due process concern that you had was somehow keyed to the concept of reasonable doubt. Maybe I misunderstood you there.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Due process would be, under Winship, keyed to reasonable doubt. But I was — trying to answer Justice Kennedy's question was as to a generic crime of a felony -- I believe it was originally — possibility was what implicated the reasonable doubt concern that you had raised.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: I — I do foresee the problem there, when you start crossing the lines between categories of crimes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay, and you're now saying that when there are only two possibilities, so that the worst that we might have would be six jurors feeling one method, six another. That does not have the same implications for the reasonable doubt standard.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: I'm saying that where it falls under a certain type category of crime that you wouldn't have the reasonable doubt problem. If you only have two theories, such as murder and reckless driving, then I think that you would have a problem, because what you're doing is your taking away from the jury that decision to make reasonable doubt and you're giving it to the judge. And you're talking about a preponderance of the evidence standard.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible) —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I didn't understand -- I understood you to ask -- (inaudible) I misunderstood your answer. I thought you — you overtly appealed to tradition which is okay with me. I mean, I'm big on tradition. But I thought you basically said that my generic statute is no good, because we've just never done things that way. And you say we've always done it this way for first degree murder. And I must say I have to take your word for it. I don't recall any lengthy discussion in your brief about how common this practice is in other States. Is it -- has it been done this way for a long time? Is it generally done this way in other States?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: You mean as a generic felony or as a

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no, no, no, no. Not the generic felony. First degree murder, which can include either felony murder or -- or -- or intent.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: That's — in almost all States, including the Federal Government, murder is defined. Federal statute, in fact, is almost identical to the Arizona statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And this could happen, you assert, in any State? The same conviction could possibly come in with a 6-6 split on the jury? Six thinking that a felony murder had been committed and six thinking there was -- there was intent to kill -- premeditated intent.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yeah, but the statutes may be that way, but how about the practice of submitting separate verdict forms? Do you think that's done everywhere?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: With the court the trial court actually submits the different forms? They are a — they have done that in some cases in Arizona, and the practical effect —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, and your supreme court even says it's a good idea to do it, doesn't it?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: For a different reason than what we're talking about here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, nevertheless it says go ahead and do it.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: It has suggested --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about other jurisdictions? What about in the Federal practice?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: I'm unaware of it being done in the Federal system or any other system. To carry on with that thought, the reason that the Arizona Supreme Court is doing that is so that what it's talking about is the punishment. It has a better view of what is going to happen, and it specifically states that in Smith and the other -- and the other case that we're — that happened here. So that the judge -- the sentencing judge and at the reviewing court has a better idea of how the jury concluded this, so that the trial judge can make the decision whether or not this individual was the one that either killed or intended to kill or —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And deserves the death penalty.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: That's the death penalty exclusively. Yes, it is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I just be sure we don't have a dispute about one rather fundamental thing? As I understand it in the premeditated murder, there's the element of the killing, the element of malice which can proved by the use of the weapon, and thirdly, there's premeditation. And that in the other theory, all that's necessary is to prove that there was robbery and a killing. And there's no requirement of premeditation. Is that correct?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: That is correct to a point. First, it presumes that there's two different offenses, which there aren't. But --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, no, that are two different ways of proving the same offense, and —

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Right, and if you prove first degree murder and —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What I'm really asking is can you prove the offense at issue in this case by proving robbery and that a death occurred in the course of the robbery without proving that the defendant had any intent to kill?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I just wanted to be sure.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: The statute so reads that because we're — what we're doing in all felony murder situations -- you're transferring the specific intent from the felony to satisfy the premeditation issue which all comes down — that's why they're satisfied in that way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you may not be able to impose the death penalty without proof of that element -- without proof of intent.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Without -- yes, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me — let me just get the terminology right. The common element is what? Premeditation? No.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: For first degree — for murder?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is the common element for first degree murder?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: There's killing with malice of forethought.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's the -- it's the killing with malice?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: That's the way the statute was defined at the time —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The malice may be supplied by premeditation or it may be supplied by the intent to commit the felony?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Either way. It can be express or

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: If they found that he had —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, you had to follow the law, that they could not have done that consistently with the law.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Yes. If they -- if they had been instructed that if you find that he is guilty of robbery, then you cannot find him guilty of murder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, no, I'm just saying — forget what these instructions — as a matter of Arizona law --

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- if the jury was totally convinced and properly instructed that he did -- had nothing to do with the killing except his participation in the robbery, but he was guilty of the robbery, could they — and consistently with Arizona law, find him guilty of a second degree murder?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So your position is that if you have an indictment in which you'd alleged just the felony murder aspect of the Arizona statute, there is no requirement for any lesser included offense under Beck?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: That's correct. Under Beck, under Arizona law, there is no allowable — there is no lesser included offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if they — if there was proof in the question of robbery — of course in a lot of felony murders I suppose you can prove who killed him?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if there's proof that — of the -- if the jury believes that there was not only a robbery, but this defendant killed him, would they give him second degree murder?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Not under Arizona law, no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: That the Arizona —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's the way it is?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: — court has decided that there is no lesser included offense of — for felony murder that includes second — second degree or the lesser — any homicide. There is no second degree murder as a lesser of actually murder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it's only a second degree to premeditated?

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Under the Arizona law, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Ford.

turns.name: R. WAYNE FORD
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Young, do you have rebuttal? You have 1 minute remaining.
